name: Beeswacks
classification_notes: A single-weight display sans-serif with a hand-painted, worn aesthetic drawn from vintage lettering on a candle box; clearly intended for display use rather than text setting.
summary: A single-weight display sans-serif drawn from hand-painted lettering found on an antique Columbia Wax Works candle box, expanded into a full character set with a deliberately worn quality.
description: The typeface originates from a small set of capital letters discovered on an old New York candle box, with Simple Bits filling out the remaining characters to match the worn, hand-painted character of the source material. It ships in one weight with upper and lowercase, numerals, diacritics for extended Latin coverage, alternate glyphs, and script-style discretionary ligatures for common words such as 'the', 'and' and 'for'. Best suited to logos, poster headlines, signage and branding where a rough vintage edge is appropriate.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Beeswacks as a vintage-esque display sans-serif inspired by a handful of letters found on an old box of candles from Columbia Wax Works in New York. They used those capitals as a guide to build out a full alphabet that retains the worn, hand-painted feel of the original, and suggest it for logos, posters, signage and branding.

name: Turbeau
classification_notes: An all-caps display face with curvy, near-monoline letterforms inspired by Handel Gothic and 1980s TV and synth aesthetics; increasing stroke contrast at heavier weights places it firmly in display territory.
summary: An all-caps retro-futuristic display face in three weights, inspired by Handel Gothic and the graphic language of 1980s television and synthesisers.
description: Drawn with curvy, near-monoline letterforms at Regular weight, Turbeau builds stroke contrast through Semibold and Bold to give heavier settings a more characterful presence. The family ships with Oblique cuts across all three weights, and a set of wormy alternate letterforms for M, N, W and several others adds a playful, period-appropriate eccentricity. Six styles in total, supplied as OTF and WOFF2.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Turbeau as a curvy, retro-futuristic all-caps display face inspired by the end credits of Knight Rider and the control panel typography of Sequential synthesisers — possibly Handel Gothic. Letterforms are uniform and nearly monoline at Regular, gaining character-building contrast at Semibold and Bold, with wormy alternates for letters like M, N and W, and Oblique versions of all three weights for maximum forward overdrive.

name: Clauthbound
classification_notes: A gently-stressed, uppercase-only display sans with rounded corners and vintage book-cover character; the decorative intent and restricted case make it firmly display rather than a general-purpose sans.
summary: A three-weight display sans drawn from the foil-stamped cloth book covers of the early-to-mid 20th century, with gently rounded corners and a built-in icon set of over 40 marks.
description: Inspired by gold foil pressed into cloth-bound books from the early 20th century, the typeface carries a soft, slightly worn quality through its gently stressed strokes and rounded corners. It ships in Regular, Semibold and Bold, covering uppercase letters, numerals, diacritics and a set of alternate characters alongside discretionary ligatures. Over 40 period-appropriate icons are included, designed to sit naturally alongside the letterforms for use on posters, invitations and themed editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Clauthbound as a gently-stressed display sans inspired by foil-stamped cloth book covers from the early-to-mid 20th century, evoking gold pressed into dusty fabric with slightly rounded corners from decades of printing. They position it as a friendly typeface that merges classy with character, suited to vintage posters, film titles, invitations and classic novels, and include over 40 carefully considered icons designed to complement the letterforms.

name: Parkly
classification_notes: An all-caps display sans-serif with Art Deco and WPA poster influences, designed exclusively for headline and signage use across four width variants.
summary: An all-caps display sans in four widths, drawn from WPA National Park poster lettering with a slight Art Deco roundness.
description: Eight styles cover four widths from Condensed to Extra Wide, each available in Regular and Soft variants with subtly rounded terminals. The family is intended for mixing widths within a single word, replicating the look of vintage wood-type park posters and signage. Alternate characters, arrowhead glyphs and extended Latin support broaden its utility for heritage-themed identity and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Parkly as inspired by vintage WPA National Park posters, offering an all-caps display sans in four widths — Condensed, Regular, Wide, and Extra Wide — each in Soft and Regular styles. Slightly rounded and subtly Art Deco, it includes alternate characters, signature arrowhead arrows, numbers, punctuation and additional language support.

name: Scorekard
classification_notes: Classified as display given its intentionally quirky, imperfect character and origin in vintage hand-lettered scoring card lettering; the designer explicitly calls it a display sans.
summary: A three-weight display sans drawn from the hand-lettering on a vintage Dennison Manufacturing Company baseball scoring card, with deliberate imperfection and inky roundness built in.
description: Inspired by lettering found on a paper baseball scoring card made by the Dennison Manufacturing Company in Massachusetts, Scorekard translates those original characters into a full upper- and lowercase alphabet across three weights: Regular, Semibold and Bold. The designer describes its character as intentionally quirky, with line wandering and friendly, inky roundness kept from becoming too eccentric to be broadly usable. A swashy ornament from the original card is included as an alternate asterisk, and Latin diacritic support extends the character set for wider language use.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Scorekard as an imperfect sans-serif inspired by vintage paper baseball scoring card lettering, with what they call Danmade line wandering and inky roundness. It ships in three weights with a full lowercase, Latin diacritic support, and a decorative alternate asterisk recreated from the original Dennison card's swashy ornament.

name: Spagetty
classification_notes: A display-weight slab serif designed around a single expressive capital R, with Western-themed ornamentation and a Rough variant; firmly display in intent and character, not suited to running text.
summary: A Spaghetti Western-themed display slab built around a curly-legged capital R, available in Regular and subtly worn Rough styles.
description: Designed around the personality of a single letterform, the capital R with its handlebar-curl leg, and expanded outward from there. The aesthetic draws on Spaghetti Western cinema, Ennio Morricone scores and 1980s cartoons, giving it a knowingly retro, saloon-door character. Two styles are included: Regular and Rough, the latter textured for use at large sizes, alongside diacritics, alternate glyphs and discretionary ligatures for common words.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Spagetty as beginning with the urge to create the quintessential slab serif capital R, with its handlebar mustache curly leg. The rest of the design followed, inspired by the Wild West, Ennio Morricone's Spaghetti Western scores, sasparilla and 80s cartoons. The family includes Regular and Rough styles, upper and lowercase, numerals, diacritics with expanded language support, and alternate glyphs including a Western sunset, star and lucky horseshoe.

name: Hubano
classification_notes: An all-caps display serif with highly decorative, zig-zag crossbars on key letters, inspired by hand-lettered Cuban cigar box typography. The ornamental character and single-case design place it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: An all-caps display serif drawn from Cuban cigar box lettering, distinguished by unconventional zig-zag crossbars on the A and H.
description: Inspired by hand-lettering spotted on a cigar box, this typeface channels the craft tradition of Caribbean tobacco culture into two styles: a clean Regular and a Rough variant that mimics a hand-painted finish. The unconventional crossbars on A and H are the defining feature, with standard alternates provided for those who want something less conspicuous. Western Latin diacritics are included alongside a full set of numerals and punctuation.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Hubano as an all-caps display serif rooted in Caribbean history, inspired by lettering found on a Cuban cigar box. It comes in Regular and Rough styles, the latter evoking a hand-painted-in-Havana feel, with full A–Z, numerals, punctuation, diacritics for expanded Western language support, and alternates for the letters with the signature zig-zag crossbars.

name: Dragönsteel
classification_notes: Dragönsteel is explicitly a blackletter typeface, informed by broad nib pen construction and referencing heavy metal lettering and medieval typographic traditions, while softening the style with friendlier proportions.
summary: A broad-nib-informed blackletter in three stylistic variants, drawing on heavy metal logos and 1980s role-playing game aesthetics with a deliberately approachable character.
description: Three styles, Regular, Rough and Sharp, each built on the same broad-nib blackletter skeleton but with distinct surface qualities ranging from clean to distressed to angular. The character set includes uppercase, lowercase, numerals, Latin Extended accents, alternate glyphs, ligatures and three styles of capitals. Aimed squarely at display use in fantasy, gaming and music contexts where blackletter is expected but rigid formality is not.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Dragönsteel as their take on a modern-ish blackletter typeface, inspired by heavy metal logos, 1980s role-playing games, dragons and dusty leather-bound books. It is guided by broad nib pen forms but takes liberties to introduce a degree of friendliness to an otherwise serious style. The family ships as three styles, Regular, Rough and Sharp, with OTF and WOFF2 files, covering personal and commercial use for desktop, web and app under their Super Simple License.

name: Cartridge
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a display sans-serif, with retro geometric letterforms inspired by early 1980s Atari video game cartridge typography, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A display sans-serif drawn from the typography of Atari 2600 game cartridges, offered in five weights across standard and softened variants.
description: Inspired directly by the chunky label lettering of early 1980s Atari cartridges, the family spans five weights from Light to Black, each available in a standard and a Soft variant with rounded terminals. Alternate characters allow designers to dial in the period feel more precisely, and three pixel-art Bit Monster dingbats are included as Easter eggs. Upper and lowercase, diacritics and extended punctuation give it broader utility than a strict novelty face.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Cartridge as a new display sans-serif evoking quintessential 80s vibes, born from the designer's childhood obsession with the Atari 2600 and a recent visit to a vintage video game shop. The family includes five weights in Regular and Soft variants, alternate characters for authentic early-80s nostalgia, and three hidden Bit Monster pixel characters suited to Space Invaders or Galaga aesthetics.

name: Free Lunch
classification_notes: An all-caps display typeface with a hand-lettered, sign-painting aesthetic, offering Regular and Rough styles suited to headlines and retro-themed branding rather than body text.
summary: An all-caps display font with a mid-century sign-painting character, offered in Regular and Rough styles with over 40 discretionary ligatures.
description: Built around the visual language of butcher shops, lunch counters and deli waxed paper, this typeface leans into hand-lettered Americana without feeling laboured. Two styles, Regular and Rough, are included alongside alternate glyphs for A, E, L, T, Y and the at sign, plus an extensive set of discretionary ligatures covering combinations like TH, QU and double-letter runs. Best suited to headlines, logos and short-form packaging where a nostalgic, slightly playful tone is needed.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Free Lunch as an all-caps display font that would sit comfortably in a butcher shop window, a 1955 lunch counter menu or printed on deli waxed paper. The family includes Regular and Rough styles, A–Z, numerals, basic punctuation, a handful of alternate glyphs and over 40 ligatures for adding sign-painting character to headlines and logos.

name: Paint Factory
classification_notes: An all-caps hand-painted display face with deliberate roughness and imperfection, inspired by weathered signage lettering on a 19th-century New England building. Its angular forms and worn texture place it firmly in the vintage display category.
summary: An all-caps display typeface drawn from the weathered hand-painted lettering on a 19th-century marine paint factory in Gloucester Harbour, Massachusetts.
description: Available in Regular and Bold, it covers A–Z, numerals, basic punctuation and a handful of alternate glyphs. The deliberate roughness and angular bends of the source lettering are preserved throughout, lending it credibility in signage, athletic and collegiate contexts. It suits sweatshirt graphics, blue-collar branding or any project that calls for the character of layers of hand-applied paint.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Paint Factory as a vintage-inspired, all-caps display typeface born from the worn white-washed lettering on the old Tarr & Wonson marine paint factory building at Gloucester Harbour, built around 1863. They highlight its angular bends and imperfection as deliberate features, and suggest it works well for ironic, athletic or collegiate sweatshirt lettering and sturdy, hand-painted signage treatments.

name: Wilco Loft Sans
classification_notes: An all-caps, high contrast sans serif drawn from a specific vintage sign, with strong display character and no lowercase; classified as display due to its expressive contrast and single-case design.
summary: A custom all-caps display sans commissioned for Wilco, derived from a vintage blow-mould guitar shop sign rescued from a Chicago suburb and installed in the band's recording studio.
description: Designed for the band Wilco, this typeface traces its letterforms back to a handful of characters on an old 'GUITAR' sign hanging in The Loft, the band's Chicago recording studio. Simple Bits took those original glyphs and expanded them into a full alphabet across four weights, named Treble, Midrange, Low End and Bass. Each weight also contains custom icons representing each band member's favourite instrument, drawn in the style of the lefty acoustic on the original sign.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Wilco Loft Sans as a typeface created in collaboration with Wilco, inspired by an iconic 'GUITAR' sign hanging in The Loft, the band's Chicago recording studio. The sign originally came from Guitar Fun Inc, a suburban Chicago guitar shop, and was gifted to the studio as a birthday surprise for Jeff Tweedy. Simple Bits took the handful of letters found on the sign's blow moulds and expanded them into a full alphabet in four weights, with custom band member icons as an easter egg.

name: Vault Alarm
classification_notes: All-caps chunky display sans-serif derived from lettering found on vintage New England bank alarm boxes; quirky and irregular in character, clearly intended for display use only.
summary: An all-caps display sans drawn from lettering on vintage New England bank alarm boxes, offered in subtly-rounded Regular and Sharp cuts.
description: Based on a handful of letters spotted on old bank vault alarm boxes in New England, this chunky all-caps display face leans into the irregular charm of its source material. It ships in two styles: Regular with softly rounded terminals and Sharp with harder edges. The character set covers capitals, numerals and basic punctuation, with a handful of alternate glyphs for G, Z, the ampersand and hash.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Vault Alarm as a chunky, quirky display sans-serif inspired by lettering on old New England bank alarm boxes. It is all-caps with numbers and basic punctuation, plus bonus alternate characters, and comes in Regular (subtly rounded) and Sharp styles, both included at purchase.

name: Grandsans
classification_notes: An all-caps display typeface drawn from early-to-mid twentieth century sign-painting traditions, specifically inspired by hand-lettered baseball stadium signage; its quirky letterforms and Rough variant place it firmly in the display category.
summary: An all-caps display family drawn from 1940s baseball stadium signage, with a clean Regular and a distressed Rough variant evoking thick painted letterforms.
description: Inspired by a 1946 World Series ticket stub and the hand-lettered signage around Boston's Fenway Park, Grandsans captures the idiosyncratic quality of mid-century sign painting. The family ships in two styles, Regular and Rough, both all-caps, with a set of alternate characters and discretionary ligatures for fractional figures. Quirky letterforms in E, M and N, plus a backwards K for strikeout scorekeeping, give it a specific, period-faithful character.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Grandsans as inspired by an old 1946 World Series ticket stub, channelling the thick cream-painted letterforms found on the iron and tin signage at Fenway Park. The family includes Regular and Rough styles, both all-caps, with alternates and special glyphs designed to deliver vintage baseball atmosphere.

name: Rotundo
classification_notes: Rotundo is explicitly described as a display sans-serif with strong thick-thin contrast and rounded forms, placing it squarely in the display category with a high-contrast sans subcategory.
summary: A high-contrast, rounded display sans with a deliberately pudgy character, sold in Regular, Fat and Soft styles.
description: Thick-thin contrast and exaggerated roundness give this display sans a playful, approachable quality suited to logos, signage and short-form headline work. The family includes three styles — Regular, Fat and Soft — alongside alternate characters for A, ampersand and hash, plus a set of discretionary ligatures in the Regular weight covering common short words. Coverage extends to Latin Extended with diacritics for several European languages.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Rotundo as super-friendly and high-contrast, with a fat and round personality and thick-thin strokes. Designed for logos, signage and anything that calls for pudgy character, it ships with Regular and Fat weights plus a Soft variant, alternate characters and discretionary ligatures, and includes OTF and WOFF2 files for desktop, web and app use under their Super Simple License.

name: Easy Coast
classification_notes: An all-caps wood-type-inspired display face with groovy, flowing letterforms and two width variants; firmly in the display category with retro decorative roots.
summary: An all-caps display typeface drawn from wood-block letterpress traditions, shaped into retro, flowing forms with a surf-culture sensibility.
description: Comes in two widths, Regular and Condensed, designed to be mixed together for typographic lockups. Coverage is practical rather than broad: letters, numerals, basic punctuation and a set of alternate characters including discretionary ligatures for BY and OF. The wood-type origins are literal, with physical letterpress versions produced alongside the digital release.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Easy Coast as inspired by saltwater, waves and a certain Brady Bunch wipeout, rooted in carved wood blocks reshaped into retro, groovy, flowing letterforms. The family ships in Regular and Condensed widths, all-caps with alternate characters and a handful of symbols, and comes packaged as OTF and WOFF2 files under their Super Simple License.

name: Captain Edward
classification_notes: An all-caps display serif with chunky, rounded letterforms drawing from Cooper Black's lighter-weight relatives, designed purely for headline and decorative use rather than text setting.
summary: An all-caps display serif with pirate-themed character, offering clean and distressed variants in two weights.
description: Named after the pirate Edward Teach, better known as Blackbeard, this all-caps display serif draws on Cooper Black's lighter-weight siblings and the rugged character of the New England coast. The family ships in four styles: Regular, Bold, and textured Rough variants of each, alongside a handful of swash alternates for K, L, Q, R, X and several punctuation marks. Coverage extends to Latin Extended for basic European language support.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Captain Edward as an all-caps display serif inspired by Atlantic pirates and named after Captain Edward Teach, alias Blackbeard. They cite Cooper Black's lighter-weight siblings and the rugged New England coastline as design references, and the family includes four styles with alternate swash characters and European diacritic support.

name: Ships Whistle
classification_notes: A monoline sans-serif designed specifically for display use, inspired by hand-painted ferry signage; the clean and rough variants reinforce its display and signage-oriented character.
summary: A monoline display sans-serif drawn from hand-lettered ferry signage on the Woods Hole to Martha's Vineyard route, available in clean and rough variants across four styles.
description: Inspired directly by lettering spotted on a Massachusetts ferry, the family comes in eight styles: Regular, Italic, Bold, and Bold Italic, each in both Clean and Rough versions. Extras include alternate character glyphs, maritime icons, swashy italic caps, and an ST/st ligature carried over from what may have been a printing error on the original sign. Best suited to signage, wayfinding and anything leaning into nautical or vintage American vernacular.
foundry_blurb: Simple Bits describe Ships Whistle as a monoline display sans-serif inspired by lettering found on the ferry from Woods Hole to Martha's Vineyard in Massachusetts. It comes in eight styles covering Clean and Rough versions of Regular, Italic, Bold, and Bold Italic, with bonus alternate glyphs, icons and a signature ST/st ligature that may have originated as a printing error on the source sign.
